feed_card.headline: Each year about 27 million tons of Saharan dust fall on the Amazon, carrying the phosphorus the rainforest needs.

teasers: How a desert keeps a rainforest fed
The dead lake behind half the dust
Why the greenest place leans on the driest

sections.content: Each year about 27 million tons of Saharan dust fall on the Amazon, carrying the phosphorus the rainforest needs.

Africa</text><path d='M122 152 C 170 124, 232 188, 276 164' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2.5' opacity='0.55' stroke-linecap='round'/><path d='M276 164 l-12 -5 m12 5 l-9 9' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2.5' opacity='0.55' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round'/><circle cx='150' cy='141' r='2' fill='currentColor' opacity='0.4'/><circle cx='200' cy='158' r='2' fill='currentColor' opacity='0.4'/><circle cx='245' cy='168' r='2' fill='currentColor' opacity='0.4'/><text x='198' y='212' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='12' opacity='0.45' text-anchor='middle'>Atlantic</text><ellipse cx='322' cy='170' rx='46' ry='56' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='322' y='104' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace' font-size='15' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>27.7 Mt settles</text><text x='322' y='244' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>Amazon</text></svg>

sections.content: Two places that could hardly be less alike turn out to be tied together by the wind. The Sahara is the driest large desert on Earth and the Amazon the wettest great forest, and every year the first feeds the second. Storms tear mineral dust off the African desert, the trade winds carry it thousands of kilometers over the Atlantic, and a share of it drifts down onto the rainforest. The forest needs the delivery. Its own soil is too washed out to hold enough phosphorus, so the green wall of the Amazon leans, in part, on a desert it will never touch.

items: Every year roughly 27.7 million tons of Saharan dust settle on the Amazon, enough to load about 105,000 semi trucks.
Spread across the calendar, that is close to 290 truckloads of desert dust drifting down onto the rainforest every single day.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 114 400 96' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><text x='200' y='132' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='600' opacity='0.75' text-anchor='middle'>Dust landing on the Amazon</text><text x='108' y='178' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace' font-size='22' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>27.7 Mt</text><text x='108' y='198' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='12' opacity='0.65' text-anchor='middle'>a year</text><path d='M178 172 h44' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2.5' opacity='0.6' stroke-linecap='round'/><path d='M222 172 l-10 -6 m10 6 l-10 6' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2.5' opacity='0.6' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round'/><text x='312' y='176' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace' font-size='22' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>~290</text><text x='312' y='196' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='12' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>truckloads a day</text></svg>

sections.content: For a long time the amount was a guess, because nobody could weigh a cloud strung out across an ocean. The number came into focus when NASA pointed a laser at it. The CALIPSO satellite carries a lidar that fires pulses straight down and reads the echo, slicing the dust plume into a vertical profile as it drifts west, so the haze can be mapped through its full depth instead of only seen from above. Averaging those profiles from 2007 to 2013 gave the first multiyear measurement: about 27.7 million tons reaching the Amazon basin each year, out of roughly 182 million lifted off Africa. Knowing the dust's mineral makeup, the team then estimated the phosphorus riding along with it at around 22,000 tons a year, close to what the basin loses to its rivers and rain.

content.body: Most of that dust begins in one small, dead place. Northeast of Lake Chad lies the Bodele Depression, the floor of a vast lake that dried out thousands of years ago and is now the most intense dust source on the planet. Its bed is diatomite, the chalky remains of countless microscopic algae that lived and died in the old water, packed with the phosphorus the Amazon is short of. The depression sits in a gap between two mountain ranges that pinch and quicken the wind, so on roughly two winter days in five it sends dust into the sky, well over half a million tons in a single day. In 2006 the atmospheric scientist Ilan Koren and his colleagues followed the supply line and found that this one spot, a fraction of a percent of the Sahara's area, throws up about half of all the dust that reaches the Amazon.

key_figures.name: Ilan Koren
key_figures.role: Traced the dust to one dead lake
key_figures.one_line: His 2006 study identified the Bodele Depression, a dried lakebed in Chad, as the single source of about half the mineral dust that fertilizes the Amazon.

content.image_caption: A Saharan dust cloud blowing west over the Atlantic in 2000; this plume reached South America within days.

content.title: The forest runs on a phosphorus budget that nearly breaks even
content.body: Phosphorus is the nutrient the Amazon runs short of, and the rainforest loses it steadily as rain and floods wash it down the rivers and out to sea. The Saharan dust arrives carrying close to the same amount the basin loses, about 22,000 tons a year in, roughly 22,000 tons a year out. The forest is not banking a surplus. It is being topped up at almost exactly the rate it drains, which is why the dust matters so much and why a change at either end could tip the balance.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 112 400 128' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><text x='200' y='130' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='600' opacity='0.75' text-anchor='middle'>Phosphorus, in and out</text><text x='40' y='152' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' font-weight='700' text-anchor='start'>delivered by dust</text><rect x='40' y='158' width='280' height='28' rx='8' fill='var(--accent)' fill-opacity='0.16' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='312' y='177' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace' font-size='14' font-weight='700' text-anchor='end'>~22,000 t</text><text x='40' y='204' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.8' text-anchor='start'>washed out by rain</text><rect x='40' y='210' width='280' height='28' rx='8' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.55'/><text x='312' y='229' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace' font-size='14' font-weight='700' opacity='0.8' text-anchor='end'>~22,000 t</text></svg>

content.title: A wetter Sahel means a hungrier Amazon
content.body: The supply is not steady from year to year, and the reason lies back in Africa. When the satellite team lined up their seven years of data, the dust reaching the Amazon dropped in the years that followed a rainier wet season in the Sahel, the semi-arid belt along the desert's southern edge. More rain there grows more vegetation, which pins down the soil and leaves less loose sand for the wind to lift. So the rainforest's mineral ration is tied to rainfall a continent away, and a shifting climate could nudge that supply up or down in ways still being worked out.

sections.content: The lesson runs past one forest. No ecosystem is sealed off from the rest of the world. The Amazon runs partly on a mineral subsidy shipped across an ocean from a dead African lake, and were that supply to fail, the richest forest on Earth would slowly go hungry. The planet's parts are plumbed together in ways that pay no attention to the maps, deserts feeding forests, ancient lakebeds feeding living canopies, one place's dust becoming another's nutrient. The health of a forest can hang on something happening five thousand kilometers away, on a wind that has blown the same way for far longer than the trees have stood.

content.question: Why does the Amazon, for all its lushness, depend on dust from across an ocean?
options: Heavy rain leaches phosphorus from its soil, and the dust replaces it
Its trees cannot photosynthesize without help
The dust shields the canopy from harmful sunlight
The forest cannot get enough rainfall on its own
content.explanation: Constant rain strips nutrients, phosphorus most of all, out of the old Amazon soil. The Saharan dust brings in roughly the amount of phosphorus lost each year, keeping the forest supplied.

content.question: Of the roughly 182 million tons of dust that leave the Sahara each year, how much reaches the Amazon?
options: Almost all of it
About half
Around 28 million tons, roughly a seventh
Almost none
content.explanation: CALIPSO measured about 27.7 million tons settling over the Amazon basin, near fifteen percent of what leaves Africa. The rest falls into the ocean or carries on to the Caribbean and beyond.

content.question: Most of the dust comes from the Bodele Depression. Why is one small spot so productive?
options: It is the hottest place anywhere in the Sahara
It is the single largest desert region on Earth
Volcanoes there throw fine ash into the air
It is a dried lakebed of phosphorus-rich diatomite set in a wind-funneling gap between mountains
content.explanation: The Bodele is the bed of an ancient lake, floored with the chalky remains of algae that are loaded with phosphorus, and two mountain ranges squeeze the wind across it, lifting dust on roughly two winter days in five.

content.question: How did scientists measure how much dust reaches the Amazon?
options: By collecting it in buckets spread across the rainforest
By weighing the Sahara at the start and end of each year
With a satellite lidar that profiled the dust plume in three dimensions over seven years
By counting dust storms reported from the ground
content.explanation: NASA's CALIPSO satellite used a downward laser to slice the plume and map it in three dimensions as it crossed the Atlantic from 2007 to 2013, giving the first multiyear estimate of how much settled over the basin.

content.question: Dust reaching the Amazon dropped in years that followed a wetter Sahel. What best explains that?
options: Rain in the Sahel dissolves the dust before it can travel
More rain grows more vegetation, which holds the soil and exposes less of it to the wind
Wet years push the trade winds away from the Amazon
Rain makes the dust grains too heavy to lift
content.explanation: A wetter Sahel greens up and anchors its soil, leaving less loose sand for the wind to carry. The link ties the Amazon's dust supply to rainfall thousands of kilometers away.

content.question: What broader idea does the Sahara-to-Amazon link illustrate?
options: Rainforests would be healthier with no dust at all
Deserts and forests are entirely separate systems
Ecosystems can depend on materials carried to them across oceans and continents
The Amazon could easily make up the lost phosphorus by itself
content.explanation: The forest's nutrient budget is balanced in part by a desert an ocean away. Distant, seemingly unrelated places turn out to be linked by wind and the movement of materials around the planet.

content.myth: A rainforest must sit on rich soil to grow so lush.
content.reality: Amazon soils are old and poor. The forest survives by recycling nutrients tightly and topping up from outside sources like Saharan dust, while the ground itself stays thin.

content.myth: Trans-Atlantic dust is just haze and pollution.
content.reality: The same plume that hazes Caribbean skies is a nutrient delivery the Amazon leans on, carrying the phosphorus its rivers wash away.

content.body: The headline figures are a seven-year average, and the flow swings widely from year to year, so how the long-run balance behaves is not settled. The deposition estimate carries real uncertainty, and how much the forest truly leans on dust versus its own tight recycling is still being measured. The biggest unknown is the future: a warming, shifting climate could change Sahel rainfall and the trade winds, raising or cutting the dust supply in ways nobody can yet pin down.
items: How stable is the dust supply over decades, and how would a changing climate alter it?
How much of the Amazon's phosphorus budget comes from dust, and how much from its own internal recycling?

content.label: NASA Satellite Reveals How Much Saharan Dust Feeds Amazon's Plants. NASA Goddard 2015 (182 Mt leaves the Sahara; 27.7 Mt reaches the Amazon; 22,000 t phosphorus; CALIPSO 2007-2013)

Yu H et al. The fertilizing role of African dust in the Amazon rainforest. Geophysical Research Letters 2015;42:1984-1991 (multiyear CALIPSO estimate; dust anticorrelated with prior-year Sahel rainfall; open full text)
Koren I et al. The Bodele depression: a single spot in the Sahara that provides most of the mineral dust to the Amazon forest. Environmental Research Letters 2006;1:014005
Amazon Rain Forest Nourished by African Dust. Eos (AGU) 2015 (phosphorus-poor Amazon soil replenished by Saharan dust)
Bodele Depression. Wikipedia (dried bed of a former lake, diatomite, the most intense dust source on Earth)
